narration.default: Last tutorial, your if checked ONE thing — age >= 10. But real decisions often need more. A theme-park ride might need "tall enough AND has a ticket". A free coupon might accept "adult OR has a voucher". Today you'll learn the three small words Python uses to combine conditions: and, or, not. Each one is a standard Python keyword.

steps.title: Truthy and Falsy — what Python treats as yes/no

narration.default: Python has True and False — but inside an if, it's actually more flexible than that. A few values count as 'falsy' — Python treats them as False even though they aren't the word False. Those are: 0, the empty text "", an empty list [], an empty dict {}, and None. Everything else — any non-zero number, any non-empty text, any non-empty list — is 'truthy' and counts as True. So if my_list: works as 'is the list not empty?'. Useful to remember as you write more if-checks.

Type these three lines:

age = 15
has_pass = True
vip = False

age holds a number like before. has_pass and vip hold True and False — Python's two special yes/no values. They're called booleans after the mathematician George Boole, who first wrote down the rules of true/false logic. You'll use these three variables in every if below.

steps.title: AND — both sides must be true

narration.default:
Leave a blank line, then type:

if age > 10 and has_pass:
    print("Allowed (AND)")

Read it in English: "if age is more than 10 AND has_pass is True". Both halves must be true for the whole thing to be true. 15 > 10 is true, and has_pass is True — so Python prints the line. If has_pass were False, Python would skip even though the age check passed.

steps.title: OR — at least one side must be true

narration.default:
Type next:

if age > 18 or has_pass:
    print("Allowed (OR)")

In English: "if the kid is older than 18 OR has a pass". With or, only ONE side needs to be true. age > 18 is false (15 is not over 18), but has_pass is True — so the whole thing is true and the line prints. or is lenient; and is strict.

steps.title: OR — at least one side must be true — Visual

narration.default: or is the lenient operator — it's True if EITHER side is true. Like sharing one umbrella: as long as one of you brought it, you both stay dry.

Type next:

if not has_pass:
    print("No pass")

not flips the value. If has_pass is True, not has_pass is False. If has_pass is False, not has_pass is True. Right now has_pass is True, so not has_pass is False and nothing prints. Change has_pass to False on line 2 and run again — the line will print.

steps.title: NOT — flip true and false — Visual

narration.default: not is the flip switch. It turns True into False and False into True — the opposite of whatever it's given.
Parentheses group conditions so Python knows which ones belong together — just like in math. They make the order of and / or / not clear at a glance.

steps.title: Run and watch all the decisions unfold

narration.default: Click Run. The terminal prints Allowed (AND) and Allowed (OR) — and Access denied at the bottom. The not line stayed silent because has_pass is True. Try changing values on lines 1-3 and running again: make vip = True and Access will flip to allowed; make has_pass = False and the AND check will stop passing.

You now have and, or, not. With these three words you can express almost any rule a game or app would need. A Rock Paper Scissors judge uses and chains to spot who wins. A login screen uses and to require both username and password. A discount rule uses or to accept either a coupon or a member card. Keep them in your head: and is strict, or is lenient, not flips, parentheses group.
